subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 11! In this unit you will explore the world of three-dimensional shapes – the solid objects that fill the space all around you. Every box of dates in the Hargeisa market, every water tank on a rooftop in Mogadishu, every football kicked in the street, and every brick in the walls of the masjid is a 3D shape. Unlike the flat 2D shapes you have studied before, 3D shapes have length, width and height. They take up real space, they hold volume, and they have surfaces you could paint or wrap.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the standardized Cambridge Stage 6 workbook source rows for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you set the speed. Read each explanation slowly, copy the diagrams onto paper in your own hand, and work every example yourself before you look at the answer. Your AI tutor is available day and night to explain a step in a new way, invent extra practice, or check your working. Your teacher will meet you about twice a week to help with anything that still feels difficult. Nobody is rushing you – understanding matters far more than speed.

Why does this matter? Because three-dimensional thinking is one of the most useful skills you can build. When Hassan helps his family design a shop counter, when Amina works out how much material is needed to cover a storage box, or when Yusuf loads crates of mangoes onto a lorry so they fit without wasted space, they are all using the mathematics of 3D shapes. The ability to picture solids in your mind, to break them apart into simpler pieces, and to measure them accurately will serve you in science, in design, in trade, and in daily life.

As you work, keep a pencil, a ruler and some paper beside you. Geometry is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Sketch each shape, fold a real net out of card, and hold everyday objects in your hands as you count their faces. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it is simply a signpost showing exactly what to practise next. Let us begin exploring the solid world.
Name and describe the common 3D shapes and their properties.
Count the faces, edges and vertices of a solid accurately.
Recognise how simple shapes combine to form compound 3D shapes.
Draw and identify nets for cubes, cuboids, prisms and pyramids.
Calculate the surface area of cubes and cuboids from their nets or formulas.
Calculate the volume of cubes and cuboids using V = length × width × height.
Solve real-world problems involving surface area and volume.

concepts.title: What Makes a Shape 3D?
concepts.explanation: A three-dimensional (3D) shape has three measurements: length, width and height. This is what separates it from a flat 2D shape like a square or a circle, which has only length and width. Because a 3D shape has that third measurement, it takes up space in the real world and it has an inside as well as an outside. A drawing of a square on paper is 2D; a real dice you can pick up and turn over is 3D. Every solid shape (called a polyhedron when all its faces are flat) can be described using three key features. Learn…
concepts.example: Faces – the flat surfaces that make up the shape. Each face is itself a 2D shape, such as a square or a triangle.

concepts.title: The Common 3D Shapes
concepts.explanation: Let us meet the family of solids you need to know well. For each one, picture a real object from around your home or town, and learn its properties. A table like this is worth reading several times until the numbers become automatic. The cube and the cuboid are the two shapes you will work with most in this unit. They are closely related: both have 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices. The difference is that a cube's faces are all identical squares, while a cuboid (also called a rectangular prism) has rectangular…
concepts.example: A prism is any solid that has the same cross-section all the way along its length. If you were to slice a triangular prism straight across at any point, you would always see the same triangle. A cuboid is really a…

concepts.title: Compound 3D Shapes
concepts.explanation: Most real objects are not single simple solids – they are compound shapes made by joining two or more basic solids together. Learning to break a complicated object into simple pieces is one of the most powerful skills in geometry, because once you can see the pieces, you can measure each one and add the results. Think about a typical house. The main building is a cuboid, and the roof on top is a triangular prism. Put them together and you have described the whole house with just two familiar shapes. A tall tower…
concepts.example: To describe a compound shape clearly, use a simple four-step method:

concepts.title: Nets of 3D Shapes
concepts.explanation: A net is a flat, 2D pattern that folds up to make a 3D shape. If you took a cardboard box, carefully cut along some of its edges, and unfolded it until it lay flat on the table, the flat pattern you would see is the net of that box. Nets are the bridge between the 2D world of paper and the 3D world of solids, and they matter enormously in real life: every cereal box, milk carton and shipping crate begins life as a flat net that a machine folds and glues into shape. Let us look at the nets of the shapes you know:
concepts.example: Cube net – six squares joined edge to edge. The most familiar arrangement is a cross or plus shape: a vertical line of four squares with one square sticking out on each side of the second square. Amazingly, there are 11…

concepts.title: Surface Area
concepts.explanation: Surface area is the total area of all the faces of a 3D shape added together. It tells you how much material you would need to cover the outside of an object completely – how much paint to coat a wall, how much card to make a box, or how much wrapping paper to cover a gift. Surface area is measured in square units, such as square centimetres (cm²) or square metres (m²), because it is an area. The easiest and safest way to find surface area is to think about the net. Because the net shows every face flat, you…
concepts.example: A cube has 6 identical square faces. If each edge is 4 cm, then each square face has area 4 × 4 = 16 cm². There are 6 of them, so the total surface area is 6 × 16 = 96 cm². This gives us a neat formula: Surface area of…

concepts.title: Volume of Cubes and Cuboids
concepts.explanation: Volume is the amount of space inside a 3D shape – how much it can hold. While surface area covers the outside, volume fills the inside. Volume is measured in cubic units, such as cubic centimetres (cm³) or cubic metres (m³), because it fills three dimensions at once. One cubic centimetre is the space taken up by a tiny cube measuring 1 cm along every edge. Picture filling a cuboid box with little 1 cm cubes. Suppose the box is 5 cm long, 3 cm wide and 2 cm high. Along the bottom you could fit 5 × 3 = 15 cubes in a…
concepts.example: A cube is just a cuboid with all three measurements equal, so its volume formula is even simpler: Volume of a cube = edge × edge × edge = edge³. A storage cube with 4 cm edges has volume 4 × 4 × 4 = 64 cm³. Notice the…

methods.title: How to find the surface area of a cuboid
methods.example: A triangular prism is used as a tent shape. How many faces, edges and vertices does it have, and does it satisfy Euler's rule?
steps: Identify the three different faces: l×w, l×h and w×h.
Work out the area of each of the three faces.
Each face appears twice, so add them and double: 2(lw + lh + wh).
Write the answer in square units (cm² or m²).

methods.title: How to find the volume of a cuboid
methods.example: Hassan has a flat pattern made of 1 square and 4 identical triangles, one triangle joined to each side of the square. What solid does it fold into?
steps: Write down the length, width and height in the same units.
Multiply length × width to find the area of the base.
Multiply that result by the height.
Write the answer in cubic units (cm³ or m³).

methods.title: How to describe a compound shape
methods.example: A company makes cubic gift boxes with edges of 10 cm. How much card is needed for the surface of one box?
steps: Find the main (largest) solid.
Find the smaller solids attached to it.
Describe how they are joined (on top, beside, inside).

methods.title: Surface area of a cuboid
methods.example: A shoe box measures 20 cm long, 15 cm wide and 10 cm high. How much wrapping paper is needed to cover it exactly?
steps: Solution: Use 2(lw + lh + wh).
So 1300 cm² of paper covers the box with none spare.

methods.title: Volume of a cuboid
methods.example: A crate for carrying mangoes is 60 cm long, 40 cm wide and 30 cm high. What is its volume?
steps: Solution: Volume = length × width × height = 60 × 40 × 30.
Because 1000 cm³ = 1 litre, the crate holds 72 litres of space.

methods.title: Volume of a cube
methods.example: A storage cube has edges of 5 cm. Find its volume, and also its surface area, and explain why the units differ.
steps: Solution: Volume = edge³ = 5 × 5 × 5 = 125 cm³ (cubic units, because it fills space).
Surface area = 6 × edge² = 6 × 25 = 150 cm² (square units, because it covers the outside).
Volume uses cubic units and surface area uses square units – always keep them separate.

workedExamples.title: Count faces, edges and vertices
workedExamples.prompt: A triangular prism is used as a tent shape. How many faces, edges and vertices does it have, and does it satisfy Euler's rule?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A triangular prism has 2 triangular faces plus 3 rectangular faces, giving 5 faces. It has 9 edges (3 around each triangle makes 6, plus 3 joining them). It has 6 vertices (3 at each triangular end). Check Euler: V − E + F = 6 − 9 + 5 = 2. Correct, so our counting is right.

workedExamples.title: Identify a net
workedExamples.prompt: Hassan has a flat pattern made of 1 square and 4 identical triangles, one triangle joined to each side of the square. What solid does it fold into?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The square becomes the base and the four triangles fold up to meet at a single point, so this is the net of a square-based pyramid. Its 4 triangular faces meet at one apex above the square base.

workedExamples.title: Surface area of a cube
workedExamples.prompt: A company makes cubic gift boxes with edges of 10 cm. How much card is needed for the surface of one box?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Each square face is 10 × 10 = 100 cm². A cube has 6 faces, so the surface area is 6 × 100 = 600 cm². Using the formula 6 × edge² gives the same result: 6 × 10² = 600 cm².

workedExamples.title: Surface area of a cuboid
workedExamples.prompt: A shoe box measures 20 cm long, 15 cm wide and 10 cm high. How much wrapping paper is needed to cover it exactly?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Use 2(lw + lh + wh). That is 2(20×15 + 20×10 + 15×10) = 2(300 + 200 + 150) = 2(650) = 1300 cm². So 1300 cm² of paper covers the box with none spare.

workedExamples.title: Volume of a cuboid
workedExamples.prompt: A crate for carrying mangoes is 60 cm long, 40 cm wide and 30 cm high. What is its volume?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Volume = length × width × height = 60 × 40 × 30. First 60 × 40 = 2400, then 2400 × 30 = 72 000 cm³. Because 1000 cm³ = 1 litre, the crate holds 72 litres of space.

workedExamples.title: Volume of a cube
workedExamples.prompt: A storage cube has edges of 5 cm. Find its volume, and also its surface area, and explain why the units differ.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Volume = edge³ = 5 × 5 × 5 = 125 cm³ (cubic units, because it fills space). Surface area = 6 × edge² = 6 × 25 = 150 cm² (square units, because it covers the outside). Volume uses cubic units and surface area uses square units – always keep them separate.

workedExamples.title: Only some faces
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangular swimming pool is 25 m long, 10 m wide and 2 m deep. Tiles are needed for the bottom and the four sides, but not the top (the water surface). What area must be tiled?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Bottom = 25 × 10 = 250 m². The two long sides are 25 × 2 = 50 m² each, giving 100 m². The two short sides are 10 × 2 = 20 m² each, giving 40 m². Total = 250 + 100 + 40 = 390 m². We left out the top face because it is open water.

workedExamples.title: A painted-cube puzzle
workedExamples.prompt: A 3 × 3 × 3 cube (27 small cubes) is painted red on the outside, then split apart. How many small cubes have exactly 2 red faces?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Cubes with exactly 2 red faces sit along the edges of the big cube but are not corners. Each of the 12 edges of a cube has one such middle cube, so there are 12 small cubes with exactly 2 red faces. (For the record: 8 corners have 3 faces, 6 face-centres have 1 face, and 1 hidden centre has 0 – and 8 + 12 + 6 + 1 = 27.)

workedExamples.title: Guided example 9
workedExamples.prompt: Find the volume of a cuboid 10 cm × 6 cm × 4 cm.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 10
workedExamples.prompt: A cube net is made of 6 identical squares. What 3D shape does it form, and how many different cube nets exist?

workedExamples.title: Guided example 11
workedExamples.prompt: A cuboid is 12 cm × 8 cm × 5 cm. Find both its surface area and its volume.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 12
workedExamples.prompt: A cube has a volume of 64 cm³. What is the length of one edge, and what is its surface area?

activities.title: 3D Shape Detective
activities.materials: your eyes, a notebook, and objects around your home.
steps: Hunt for 5 cubes or cuboids (boxes, books, tins of matches), 3 cylinders (tins, tubes), and 2 spheres (a ball, an orange).
For each object, record how many faces, edges and vertices it has.
Note what 2D shapes make up its faces (squares, rectangles, circles).
Choose one object and check its counts with Euler's rule: V − E + F = 2.
Keep your notebook – it becomes a personal catalogue of the solids around you.

activities.title: Net Construction Workshop
activities.materials: an empty cardboard box (a cereal or tea box is ideal), scissors, a ruler.
steps: Carefully cut along some edges of the box and unfold it until it lies flat.
You are now looking at the box's net. Count the rectangles – there should be 6, in 3 matching pairs.
Measure each rectangle and work out the surface area of the whole box.
Fold it back up to check your net really does make the box, then try drawing a different net for the same box.

activities.title: Surface Area vs Volume Laboratory
activities.materials: a small box or tin, a ruler, paper for calculations.
steps: Measure the length, width and height of your box in centimetres.
Calculate its surface area using 2(lw + lh + wh) – this is how much paper would cover it.
Calculate its volume using length × width × height – this is how much it holds.
Write both answers with their units and explain to a family member why one is in cm² and the other in cm³.

activities.title: Build a Cube from Sugar Cubes
activities.materials: small identical cubes (sugar cubes, dice, or paper cubes you make).
steps: Build a 2 × 2 × 2 cube. How many small cubes did you use? (Answer: 8.)
Build a 3 × 3 × 3 cube. How many small cubes now? (Answer: 27.) Notice the pattern: edge³.
Imagine painting the outside of your 3 × 3 × 3 cube. Predict how many small cubes would have 3, 2, 1 and 0 painted faces.
Take the cube apart to check your prediction (8 corners, 12 edges, 6 centres, 1 middle).

activities.title: Packaging Design Challenge (Family Task)
activities.materials: card, scissors, tape, a ruler, and a small object to package (a bar of soap works well).
steps: Measure the object and design a cuboid box that will hold it snugly.
Draw the net of your box on card, including small flaps for gluing.
Cut, fold and tape your box together, then check the object fits.
Calculate the surface area (card used) and volume (space inside). Which box design uses the least card? Discuss with your family.

activities.title: 3D Shapes in Architecture Investigation
activities.materials: a walk around your town, a notebook, or pictures of famous buildings.
steps: Look at buildings, water towers, and the masjid. Sketch each one.
Break every structure into simple solids: cuboids for walls, cylinders for towers, cones or pyramids for roofs.
Write a sentence describing each building as a compound shape, using precise shape names.
Explain why an architect might choose each shape – strength, space, or beauty.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand the difference between surface area and volume best? What was the trickiest part? Which 3D shape do you now notice most often around you? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: There is a beautiful pattern connecting these three numbers, discovered by the mathematician Leonhard Euler. For any solid with flat faces (a polyhedron), vertices − edges + faces = 2. Written shorter, V − E + F = 2. Test it on the cube: 8 − 12 + 6 = 2. It works! Test it on a square-based pyramid: it has 5 vertices, 8 edges and 5 faces, so 5 − 8 + 5 = 2. This rule is a wonderful way to check that you have counted correctly. If your three numbers do not give 2, you have miscounted something.

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: One warning that catches many learners: curved solids like spheres, cylinders and cones are not polyhedra, so Euler's rule does not apply to them, and their curved surfaces are not really 'faces' in the strict sense. A cylinder is often said to have '2 flat circular faces and 1 curved surface', but the curved surface is not a flat face and a cylinder has no true vertices. Be careful and precise when a shape has curves.

rules.title: Key idea 3
rules.text: 🤖 Ask Your AI Tutor Ask your AI tutor to name a 3D shape and quiz you on its faces, edges and vertices. Say: Give me three solids and let me check each one with Euler's rule V minus E plus F equals 2.

rules.title: Key idea 4
rules.text: A triangular prism is used as a tent shape. How many faces, edges and vertices does it have, and does it satisfy Euler's rule?

rules.title: Key idea 5
rules.text: Mixing up edges and vertices. An edge is a line; a vertex is a corner point. Count them separately and check with Euler's rule V − E + F = 2.

rules.title: Key idea 6
rules.text: Check face-edge-vertex counts with Euler's rule V − E + F = 2 for any polyhedron.

terms: A flat surface of a solid
A line where two faces meet
A flat pattern that folds into a solid
A solid with a constant cross-section
Total area of all faces (square units)
The single top point of a cone or pyramid
commonMistakes: Surface area is squared; volume is cubed
A cuboid has 6 faces in 3 matching pairs
Touching faces are hidden – do not include them

realProblems.prompt: A rice tin at the Kismaayo market is a cube with 15 cm edges. What volume of rice does it hold, and how much metal covers its surface?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A cuboid water tank is 2.5 m long, 2 m wide and 1.2 m deep. How many cubic metres of water does it hold when full, and how many litres is that?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Khadija wraps an Eid gift box measuring 25 cm × 18 cm × 12 cm. How much paper covers it exactly?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A store room is 6 m long, 5 m wide and 3 m high. Farah paints the four walls and the ceiling but not the floor. What area does she paint?
realProblems.answer: Ceiling 6×5 = 30; two walls 6×3 = 18 each (36); two walls 5×3 = 15 each (30); total = 30 + 36 + 30 = 96 m².
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Ceiling 6×5 = 30; two walls 6×3 = 18 each (36); two walls 5×3 = 15 each (30); total = 30 + 36 + 30 = 96 m².

realProblems.prompt: Omar stacks cubic boxes of edge 30 cm into a lorry space 180 cm long, 90 cm wide and 60 cm high. How many boxes fit, and what total volume do they fill?
realProblems.answer: Length 180÷30 = 6, width 90÷30 = 3, height 60÷30 = 2, so 6×3×2 = 36 boxes; each box is 30³ = 27000 cm³, so total volume = 36 × 27000 = 972 000 cm³.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Length 180÷30 = 6, width 90÷30 = 3, height 60÷30 = 2, so 6×3×2 = 36 boxes; each box is 30³ = 27000 cm³, so total volume = 36 × 27000 = 972 000 cm³.

realProblems.prompt: A cube has a volume of 64 cm³. What is the length of one edge, and what is its surface area?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A cube has a volume of 64 cm³. What is the length of one edge, and what is its surface area?
A cube of edge 4 cm is placed on top of a cube of edge 6 cm. Find the total surface area of the compound shape (the touching faces are hidden).
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Big cube 6×6² = 216; small cube 6×4² = 96; they touch over one 4×4 = 16 cm² square, hidden on both, subtract 2×16 = 32. Total = 216 + 96 − 32 = 280 cm².

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A 3 × 3 × 3 cube is painted on all faces and cut into 27 unit cubes. How many have 3, 2, 1 and 0 painted faces?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3 faces: 8 (corners); 2 faces: 12 (edges); 1 face: 6 (face centres); 0 faces: 1 (middle). Check 8+12+6+1 = 27.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Two cuboids each have a volume of 36 cm³. Cuboid A is 6 × 3 × 2 and Cuboid B is 9 × 4 × 1. Work out both surface areas and say which is larger.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A: 2(6×3 + 6×2 + 3×2) = 2(18+12+6) = 72 cm²; B: 2(9×4 + 9×1 + 4×1) = 2(36+9+4) = 98 cm². Cuboid B has the larger surface area even though both hold 36 cm³ – the flatter, longer box needs more covering.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Surface Area.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Surface area is the total area of all the faces of a 3D shape added together. It tells you how much material you would need to cover the outside of an object completely – how much paint to coat a wall, how much card to make a box, or how much wrapping paper to cover a gift. Surface area is measured in square units, such as square centimetres (cm²) or square metres (m²), because it is an area. The easiest and safest way to find surface area is to think about the net. Because the net shows every face flat, you…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Volume of Cubes and Cuboids.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Volume is the amount of space inside a 3D shape – how much it can hold. While surface area covers the outside, volume fills the inside. Volume is measured in cubic units, such as cubic centimetres (cm³) or cubic metres (m³), because it fills three dimensions at once. One cubic centimetre is the space taken up by a tiny cube measuring 1 cm along every edge. Picture filling a cuboid box with little 1 cm cubes. Suppose the box is 5 cm long, 3 cm wide and 2 cm high. Along the bottom you could fit 5 × 3 = 15 cubes in a…

A flat pattern that folds into a solid

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you set the speed. Read each explanation slowly, copy the diagrams onto paper in your own hand, and work every example yourself before you look at the answer. Your AI tutor is available day and night to explain a step in a new way, invent extra practice, or check your working. Your teacher will meet you about twice a week to help with anything that still feels difficult. Nobody is rushing you – understanding matters far more than speed.

I can why does this matter? Because three-dimensional thinking is one of the most useful skills you can build. When Hassan helps his family design a shop counter, when Amina works out how much material is needed to cover a storage box, or when Yusuf loads crates of mangoes onto a lorry so they fit without wasted space, they are all using the mathematics of 3D shapes. The ability to picture solids in your mind, to break them apart into simpler pieces, and to measure them accurately will serve you in science, in design, in trade, and in daily life.

I can as you work, keep a pencil, a ruler and some paper beside you. Geometry is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Sketch each shape, fold a real net out of card, and hold everyday objects in your hands as you count their faces. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it is simply a signpost showing exactly what to practise next. Let us begin exploring the solid world.
I can name and describe the common 3D shapes and their properties.
I can count the faces, edges and vertices of a solid accurately.
I can recognise how simple shapes combine to form compound 3D shapes.
I can draw and identify nets for cubes, cuboids, prisms and pyramids.
I can calculate the surface area of cubes and cuboids from their nets or formulas.
